topic: The verb ir, contractions al/del, and reflexive pronouns

tips.title: Al and del are Spanish's only two contractions
tips.body: A + el always becomes al, and de + el always becomes del -- Spanish only has these two forced contractions in the whole language. The one exception: if 'El' is capitalized as part of a proper name (like El Salvador), it does not contract.

tips.title: Ir is wildly irregular -- just memorize it as a set
tips.body: Voy, vas, va, vamos, van barely resemble the infinitive 'ir' at all, the same way soy, eres, es didn't look like 'ser.' Don't try to derive these forms from a pattern; learn the whole set together like a little song.

tips.title: Reflexive pronouns show the action bouncing back
tips.body: Se, me, te, and nos sit right before the verb when someone does something to themselves rather than to someone else. 'Se lava' means 'he washes himself' -- the action bounces back onto the subject instead of landing on someone or something else.

bonusCards.english: I go to the park, and you go to the store.
Are you going to the store, or are you going to the office?
Have you gone to the place of my friend?
The keys of that place are with my friend.

resources.name: SpanishDictionary.com — Reflexive Verbs and Reflexive Pronouns
